title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: Quiz 3 — Solutions
text:
Consider the trial of rolling a single six-sided die one time.

Write the sample space \(S\).
Give one outcome.
Define an event \(A\) and list the outcomes in \(A\).

Solution:

\(S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\)
One outcome: e.g., \(3\) (or any element of \(S\))
Many events work, just make sure the set you choose consists of outcomes from \(S\).. Example: \(A =\) “the roll is even” \(= \{2, 4, 6\}\)





Suppose a trial has sample space \(S\) and two events \(A\) and \(B\).

In words, what does “\(A\) and \(B\)” mean?
In words, what does “\(A\) or \(B\)” mean?
In words, what does “not \(A\)” mean?

Solution:

“\(A\) and \(B\)” means both \(A\) and \(B\) occur (the outcome is in both \(A\) and \(B\)).
“\(A\) or \(B\)” means at least one of \(A\) or \(B\) occurs (the outcome is in \(A\), in \(B\), or in both).
“not \(A\)” means \(A\) does not occur (the outcome is not in \(A\)).





Write a one-sentence interpretation for each:

\(P(A) = 0.7\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = 0.2\)
\(P(\text{not } A) = 0.1\)

Solution:

The probability that event \(A\) occurs is 0.7 (or: 70% chance that \(A\) occurs).
The probability that both \(A\) and \(B\) occur is 0.2.
The probability that \(A\) does not occur is 0.1.









State the probability rule for an event \(A\) if the outcomes in \(S\) are equally likely:
\[
P(A) = \quad ?
\]
Be sure to define what \(|A|\) and \(|S|\) mean in words.
Solution:
\[
P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|}
\]

\(|A|\) = number of outcomes in event \(A\)
\(|S|\) = number of outcomes in the sample space \(S\)





State the condition that must be true for the probability rule \(P(A) = |A|/|S|\) to be valid.
Solution: The outcomes in the sample space \(S\) must be equally likely.




Suppose a fair coin is tossed two times.

List the sample space \(S\) so that all outcomes are equally likely.
Let \(A\) be the event “at least one tail.” List the outcomes in \(A\).
Compute \(P(A)\).

Solution:

\(S = \{(H,H), (H,T), (T,H), (T,T)\}\) (or equivalently \(\{HH, HT, TH, TT\}\))
\(A = \{(H,T), (T,H), (T,T)\}\) — outcomes with at least one T
\(P(A) = |A|/|S| = 3/4\)





Suppose a fair die is rolled twice.

How many outcomes are in the sample space \(S\) written so that all outcomes are equally likely?
List the outcomes from \(S\) in the event \(C =\) “the sum is 11.”
Compute \(P(C)\).

Solution:

\(|S| = 6 \times 6 = 36\) (each roll has 6 outcomes; outcomes are ordered pairs \((i,j)\))
\(C = \{(5,6), (6,5)\}\) — the only ways to get sum 11
\(P(C) = |C|/|S| = 2/36 = 1/18\)





Three babies are randomly returned to three mothers (assume all pairings are equally likely).

How many outcomes are in the sample space?
Let \(A\) be the event “all 3 mothers get the correct baby.” Compute \(P(A)\).
Let \(B\) be the event “none of the mothers get the correct baby.” Compute \(P(B)\)

Solution:
Use notation: baby = number (1, 2, 3), mother = position (1st, 2nd, 3rd). So “123” means baby 1→mother 1, baby 2→mother 2, baby 3→mother 3 (3 correct matches); “132” means baby 1→mother 1, baby 2→mother 3, baby 3→mother 2 (1 correct); “312” means baby 1→mother 3, baby 2→mother 1, baby 3→mother 2 (0 correct).

Sample space: \(S = \{123, 132, 213, 231, 312, 321\}\), so \(|S| = 6\).
\(A = \{123\}\) (only one assignment with all 3 correct). \(P(A) = 1/6\).
\(B = \{231, 312\}\) (derangements: 231 has 1→2, 2→3, 3→1; 312 has 1→3, 2→1, 3→2). \(P(B) = 2/6 = 1/3\).









State the Addition Rule for any two events \(A\) and \(B\).
Solution:
\[
P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)
\]




A class has 65 students:

40 have backpacks
10 have hats
5 have both a backpack and a hat

Let \(A\) be the event a randomly selected student “has a backpack” and \(B\) be “has a hat.”

Compute \(P(A)\) and \(P(B)\).
Compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Use the addition rule to compute \(P(A \text{ or } B)\).

Solution:

\(P(A) = 40/65\), \(P(B) = 10/65\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = 5/65\)
\(P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B) = 40/65 + 10/65 - 5/65 = 45/65 = 9/13\)





If \(P(\text{rains at least once in July}) = 0.91\), compute:
\[
P(\text{no rain in July})
\]
Solution:
\[
P(\text{no rain in July}) = 1 - P(\text{rains at least once in July}) = 1 - 0.91 = 0.09
\]




Suppose \(A\) and \(B\) are non-overlapping (mutually exclusive) events.

What does “non-overlapping” mean in words?
What does it imply about \(P(A \text{ and } B)\)?
Write the simplified formula for \(P(A \text{ or } B)\) in this case.

Solution:

“Non-overlapping” means \(A\) and \(B\) cannot both occur at the same time (no outcome is in both \(A\) and \(B\)).
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = 0\)
\(P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B)\)









Write R code that:

creates x_num as the numeric value of 0,
creates x_int as an integer (use the L),
prints the class of each.

Solution:
x_num <- 0
x_int <- 0L
class(x_num)
class(x_int)




Write R code that:

creates a numeric vector v that contains the values 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10.
prints the length of v
creates a new vector called v_plus_one that is v with 1 added to each of it’s values.

Solution:
v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
length(v)
v_plus_one <- v + 1




Using v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10), write R code for each:

the 3rd element
the 2nd through 4th elements
everything except the 3rd element
all elements greater than 5

Solution:
v[3]
v[2:4]
v[-3]
v[v > 5]




Write R code that:

creates a 2x3 matrix m filled by column using 1:6,
prints the dimensions of m
extracts the value of m in the second row and first column
extracts the first row
extracts the second column

Solution:
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
dim(m)
m[2, 1]
m[1, ]
m[, 2]




Write R code that uses a for loop to fill a vector named squares with the squares of 1 through 5:
Expected result for squares
[1]  1  4  9 16 25
Solution:
squares <- numeric(5)
for (i in 1:5) {
  squares[i] <- i^2
}
squares




Write R code to estimate the probability that a fair die roll is even using simulation:- simulate n <- 10000 die rolls using sample(1:6, size = n, replace = TRUE) - compute the estimated probability with mean(...)Your code should create objects named n, rolls, and p_even.
Solution:
n <- 10000
rolls <- sample(1:6, size = n, replace = TRUE)
p_even <- mean(rolls %in% c(2, 4, 6))
Alternative: p_even <- mean(rolls %% 2 == 0) checks to see if the remainder when divided by 2 is 0 or not.

title: Quiz 5 — Questions and Solutions (Draft)
text:
These are the original practice problems plus solutions for checking your work.





Question: State Bayes’ rule in formula form. (You may use \(A\) and \(B\) for the two events.)
Solution: \(P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\, P(A)}{P(B)}\) (or equivalent form, e.g. with \(P(B)\) written via law of total probability).




Question: State the law of total probability for the case where the sample space is partitioned into two events \(A\) and “not \(A\).” That is, give the formula for \(P(B)\) in terms of conditional probabilities and \(P(A)\), \(P(\text{not } A)\).
Solution: \(P(B) = P(B \mid A)\, P(A) + P(B \mid \text{not } A)\, P(\text{not } A)\).







Question: Suppose the prevalence of a disease in a population is \(P(D) = 0.03\) (3%). A test has sensitivity \(P(T^+ \mid D) = 0.90\) and specificity \(P(T^- \mid \text{not } D) = 0.95\). Find: (1) \(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D)\), (2) \(P(D \text{ and } T^+)\), (3) \(P(\text{not } D \text{ and } T^+)\), (4) \(P(T^+)\), (5) \(P(D \mid T^+)\).
Solutions:

\(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D) = 1 - P(T^- \mid \text{not } D) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05\).
\(P(D \text{ and } T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D)\, P(D) = 0.90 \times 0.03 = 0.027\).
\(P(\text{not } D \text{ and } T^+) = P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D)\, P(\text{not } D) = 0.05 \times 0.97 = 0.0485\).
\(P(T^+) = P(D \text{ and } T^+) + P(\text{not } D \text{ and } T^+) = 0.027 + 0.0485 = 0.0755\).
\(P(D \mid T^+) = \dfrac{P(D \text{ and } T^+)}{P(T^+)} = \dfrac{0.027}{0.0755} \approx 0.358\) (or use Bayes’ rule).





Question: Prevalence \(P(D) = 0.01\), sensitivity \(P(T^+ \mid D) = 0.98\), specificity \(P(T^- \mid \text{not } D) = 0.90\). Find: (1) \(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D)\), (2) \(P(T^+)\), (3) \(P(D \mid T^+)\).
Solutions:

\(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10\).
\(P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D)P(D) + P(T^+ \mid \text{not } D)P(\text{not } D) = 0.98(0.01) + 0.10(0.99) = 0.0098 + 0.099 = 0.1088\).
\(P(D \mid T^+) = \dfrac{0.0098}{0.1088} \approx 0.0901\).








Question: (1) Compute \(4!\). (2) What is \(0!\) (by convention)?
Solutions: (1) \(4! = 24\). (2) \(0! = 1\).




Question: (1) Compute \(_5P_2\). (2) Compute \(_5C_2\).
Solutions: (1) \(_5P_2 = \dfrac{5!}{(5-2)!} = \dfrac{5!}{3!} = 5 \times 4 = 20\). (2) \(_5C_2 = \binom{5}{2} = \dfrac{5!}{2!\,3!} = 10\).




Question: (1) How many ways to choose 3 from 7 for a committee (order doesn’t matter)? (2) How many ways to choose 3 from 7 for three distinct roles (president, secretary, treasurer)?
Solutions: (1) \(_7C_3 = \binom{7}{3} = 35\). (2) \(_7P_3 = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210\).




Question: A team of 4 from 10 players; order doesn’t matter. (1) Write the expression. (2) Compute the value.
Solutions: (1) \(_10C_4\) or \(\binom{10}{4}\). (2) \(\binom{10}{4} = \dfrac{10!}{4!\,6!} = 210\).







Question: State the four conditions for a binomial distribution.
Solution: (1) Each trial has two outcomes (success/failure). (2) Fixed number of trials \(n\). (3) Fixed probability of success \(p\) on each trial. (4) Trials are independent.




Question: Fair coin flipped 12 times; \(X\) = number of heads. (1) What is \(n\)? (2) What does \(x\) represent? (3) Definition of \(p\) and its value here?
Solutions: (1) \(n = 12\) (number of trials). (2) \(x\) is the observed number of successes (heads). (3) \(p\) is the probability of success on a single trial; here \(p = 0.5\).




Question: 20% of seeds fail to germinate; you plant 8 seeds; \(X\) = number that fail. (1) Identify \(n\) and \(p\). (2) Write expression for \(P(X = 3)\).
Solutions: (1) \(n = 8\), \(p = 0.20\). (2) \(P(X = 3) = \binom{8}{3}(0.2)^3(0.8)^5\) or \(_8C_3\, (0.2)^3(0.8)^5\).







Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X = 5)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Store in prob_five.
Solution: prob_five <- dbinom(5, size = 10, prob = 0.4)




Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X \leq 3)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Store in prob_at_most_3.
Solution: prob_at_most_3 <- pbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4)




Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X > 2)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Use pbinom with lower.tail = FALSE. Store in prob_more_than_2.
Solution: prob_more_than_2 <- pbinom(2, size = 10, prob = 0.4, lower.tail = FALSE)




Question: Simulate 1000 values from \(\text{Binomial}(n = 8, p = 0.2)\). Store in sims.
Solution: sims <- rbinom(1000, size = 8, prob = 0.2)




Question: Using sims from E3, write one line of R code to estimate \(P(X \leq 2)\). Store in p_est.
Solution: p_est <- mean(sims <= 2)




Question: What does aes() do in a ggplot2 plot? Select the single best answer.
Solution: b. It maps variables in the data to visual properties (e.g., which column is x, which is y, color, fill).




Question: Fill in the blanks so the code creates a data frame (with x = possible numbers of heads in 10 tosses, prob = \(P(X=x)\) for \(n=10\), \(p=0.4\)) and then a bar plot with geom_col(). Options: 0:10 | dbinom(0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.4) | x | prob.
Solution:
df <- data.frame(x = 0:10, prob = dbinom(0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.4))
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) + geom_col()
Order of blanks: first row 0:10, dbinom(0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.4); second row x, prob.

title: Quiz 5 — Questions and Solutions (Draft)
text:
Question: (1) Compute \(4!\). (2) What is \(0!\) (by convention)?
Solutions: (1) \(4! = 24\). (2) \(0! = 1\).




Question: (1) Compute \(_5P_2\). (2) Compute \(_5C_2\).
Solutions: (1) \(_5P_2 = \dfrac{5!}{(5-2)!} = \dfrac{5!}{3!} = 5 \times 4 = 20\). (2) \(_5C_2 = \binom{5}{2} = \dfrac{5!}{2!\,3!} = 10\).




Question: (1) How many ways to choose 3 from 7 for a committee (order doesn’t matter)? (2) How many ways to choose 3 from 7 for three distinct roles (president, secretary, treasurer)?
Solutions: (1) \(_7C_3 = \binom{7}{3} = 35\). (2) \(_7P_3 = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210\).




Question: A team of 4 from 10 players; order doesn’t matter. (1) Write the expression. (2) Compute the value.
Solutions: (1) \(_10C_4\) or \(\binom{10}{4}\). (2) \(\binom{10}{4} = \dfrac{10!}{4!\,6!} = 210\).

title: Quiz 5 — Questions and Solutions (Draft)
text:
Question: State the four conditions for a binomial distribution.
Solution: (1) Each trial has two outcomes (success/failure). (2) Fixed number of trials \(n\). (3) Fixed probability of success \(p\) on each trial. (4) Trials are independent.




Question: Fair coin flipped 12 times; \(X\) = number of heads. (1) What is \(n\)? (2) What does \(x\) represent? (3) Definition of \(p\) and its value here?
Solutions: (1) \(n = 12\) (number of trials). (2) \(x\) is the observed number of successes (heads). (3) \(p\) is the probability of success on a single trial; here \(p = 0.5\).




Question: 20% of seeds fail to germinate; you plant 8 seeds; \(X\) = number that fail. (1) Identify \(n\) and \(p\). (2) Write expression for \(P(X = 3)\).
Solutions: (1) \(n = 8\), \(p = 0.20\). (2) \(P(X = 3) = \binom{8}{3}(0.2)^3(0.8)^5\) or \(_8C_3\, (0.2)^3(0.8)^5\).

title: Quiz 5 — Questions and Solutions (Draft)
text:
Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X = 5)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Store in prob_five.
Solution: prob_five <- dbinom(5, size = 10, prob = 0.4)




Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X \leq 3)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Store in prob_at_most_3.
Solution: prob_at_most_3 <- pbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4)




Question: Write one line of R code to compute \(P(X > 2)\) when \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 10, p = 0.4)\). Use pbinom with lower.tail = FALSE. Store in prob_more_than_2.
Solution: prob_more_than_2 <- pbinom(2, size = 10, prob = 0.4, lower.tail = FALSE)




Question: Simulate 1000 values from \(\text{Binomial}(n = 8, p = 0.2)\). Store in sims.
Solution: sims <- rbinom(1000, size = 8, prob = 0.2)




Question: Using sims from E3, write one line of R code to estimate \(P(X \leq 2)\). Store in p_est.
Solution: p_est <- mean(sims <= 2)




Question: What does aes() do in a ggplot2 plot? Select the single best answer.
Solution: b. It maps variables in the data to visual properties (e.g., which column is x, which is y, color, fill).




Question: Fill in the blanks so the code creates a data frame (with x = possible numbers of heads in 10 tosses, prob = \(P(X=x)\) for \(n=10\), \(p=0.4\)) and then a bar plot with geom_col(). Options: 0:10 | dbinom(0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.4) | x | prob.
Solution:
df <- data.frame(x = 0:10, prob = dbinom(0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.4))

title: Quiz 7 — Solutions
text:
Solutions for checking your work. Cover Lecture 17: Type I and Type II errors, power, and balancing errors.





Question: Define the significance level (often denoted \(\alpha\)) in the context of hypothesis testing.
Solution: The significance level \(\alpha\) is the maximum probability of making a Type I error we are willing to accept. AND it’s the cutoff for making a decision: we reject \(H_0\) when the p-value is less than \(\alpha\). For example, if \(\alpha = 0.05\), we reject \(H_0\) when p-value \(< 0.05\), and the probability of incorrectly rejecting a true \(H_0\) is controlled at (at most) 0.05.




Question: Define a Type I error in the context of hypothesis testing.
Solution: A Type I error is rejecting the null hypothesis \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true. It is a “false alarm” — we conclude there is an effect or difference when there isn’t.




Question: Define a Type II error in the context of hypothesis testing.
Solution: A Type II error is failing to reject the null hypothesis \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually false. It is a “missed opportunity” — we fail to detect a real effect or difference.







Question: We want to know if a coin lands on heads more often than not. Let \(p\) = probability of heads. (1) State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\). (2) What would a Type I error be in context? (3) What would a Type II error be in context?
Solutions:

\(H_0: p = 0.5\) (the coin is fair). \(H_a: p > 0.5\) (the coin is “lucky” for heads).
Type I error: Concluding that the coin is lucky for heads (rejecting \(H_0\)) when it is actually fair. We falsely claim the coin favors heads.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the coin is lucky for heads (failing to reject \(H_0\)) when the coin actually does favor heads. We miss detecting a biased coin.





Question: We want to know if a die lands on the face “6” more often than it should. Let \(p\) = probability of a 6. (1) State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\). (2) Type I error in context? (3) Type II error in context?
Solutions:

\(H_0: p = 1/6\) (the die is fair). \(H_a: p > 1/6\) (the die is “lucky” for 6).
Type I error: Concluding that the die is lucky for 6 when it is actually fair. We falsely claim the die is biased.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the die is lucky for 6 when it really is biased toward 6. We miss detecting the bias.





Question: We want to know if a new treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases. Let \(p\) = proportion of patients for whom the treatment is effective. (1) State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\). (2) Type I error in context? (3) Type II error in context?
Solutions:

\(H_0: p = 0.10\) (or \(p \leq 0.10\)); \(H_a: p > 0.10\) (the treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases).
Type I error: Concluding that the treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases when it actually isn’t. We might adopt an ineffective treatment (false discovery).
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the treatment is effective when it actually helps more than 10% of patients. We might abandon a beneficial treatment (missed opportunity).





Question: We want to know if the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than the national rate of 10%. Let \(p\) = proportion at our school who are left-handed. (1) State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\). (2) Type I error in context? (3) Type II error in context?
Solutions:

\(H_0: p = 0.10\); \(H_a: p > 0.10\).
Type I error: Concluding that the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than 10% when it actually equals (or is not higher than) 10%. We falsely claim our school has a higher left-handed rate.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the proportion is higher than 10% when it actually is. We miss detecting that our school has a higher left-handed rate than the national rate.





Question: We want to know if the proportion of defective items from a new supplier is less than 15%. Let \(p\) = proportion from this supplier that are defective. (1) State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\). (2) Type I error in context? (3) Type II error in context?
Solutions:

\(H_0: p = 0.15\) (or \(p \geq 0.15\)); \(H_a: p < 0.15\).
Type I error: Concluding that the defect rate is less than 15% when it actually is 15% or higher. We might wrongly approve a supplier with a high defect rate.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the defect rate is less than 15% when it actually is lower. We might wrongly reject a good supplier.






Cutoff: \(\alpha = 0.05\).


Setup: \(H_0: p = 0.5\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.5\). Data: 62 heads in 100 flips. P-value = 0.0096.
Solutions:

Reject or fail to reject? P-value (0.0096) \(< 0.05\), so we reject \(H_0\).
Could a Type I error be being made? Yes. A Type I error occurs when we reject \(H_0\) and \(H_0\) is actually true. We did reject \(H_0\); we don’t know whether \(H_0\) is true or false, so it is possible we rejected a true null (fair coin).
Could a Type II error be being made? No. A Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. We did reject \(H_0\), so we cannot have made a Type II error.
Type I error in context: Concluding that the coin is biased toward heads when it is actually fair. We would have falsely claimed the coin is “lucky” for heads.





Setup: \(H_0: p = 0.10\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.10\). Data: 4 left-handed in 25 students. P-value = 0.21.
Solutions:

Reject or fail to reject? P-value (0.21) \(> 0.05\), so we fail to reject \(H_0\).
Could a Type I error be being made? No. A Type I error is rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is true. We did not reject \(H_0\), so we cannot have made a Type I error.
Could a Type II error be being made? Yes. A Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. We failed to reject \(H_0\); if in reality the proportion of left-handed students at our school is greater than 10%, we would have missed detecting that (Type II error).
Type II error in context: Failing to conclude that the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than the national 10% when it actually is higher. We would have missed detecting that our school has a higher left-handed rate.





Setup: \(H_0: p = 0.15\) vs \(H_a: p < 0.15\). Data: 3 defective in 40 items. P-value = 0.034.
Solutions:

Reject or fail to reject? P-value (0.034) \(< 0.05\), so we reject \(H_0\).
Could a Type I error be being made? Yes. We rejected \(H_0\). If the true defect rate is actually 15% (or higher), we would have made a Type I error — concluding the rate is less than 15% when it isn’t.
Could a Type II error be being made? No. We rejected \(H_0\), so we did not “fail to reject”; a Type II error cannot occur.
Type I error in context: Concluding that the proportion of defective items from this supplier is less than 15% when it actually is 15% or higher. We would have wrongly approved (or been too optimistic about) a supplier whose defect rate is not below 15%.








Question: Which would help reduce the probability of a Type I error?
Solution: B. Use a smaller significance level (e.g. \(\alpha = 0.01\) instead of 0.05). Then we require stronger evidence (smaller p-value) before rejecting \(H_0\), so we are less likely to reject a true \(H_0\). Increasing or decreasing sample size does not change the Type I error rate for a fixed \(\alpha\); changing \(\alpha\) does.




Question: Assume \(\alpha\) is fixed. What can we do to reduce the Type II error rate (increase power)?
Solution: C. Increase the sample size. With a larger \(n\), we have more information, so we are more likely to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false (higher power, lower Type II error). We are not allowed to change \(\alpha\), so A and B are ruled out. Decreasing the sample size would decrease power (increase Type II error), so D is wrong.

title: Quiz 4 — Solutions
text:
In words, what does \(P(A \mid B)\) mean?
State the formula for \(P(A \mid B)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)).

Solution:

\(P(A \mid B)\) means the probability that event \(A\) occurs given that event \(B\) has occurred.
\(P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}\)






State the multiplication rule for \(P(A \text{ and } B)\) in terms of \(P(A \mid B)\) and \(P(B)\).
State the special case when \(A\) and \(B\) are independent.

Solution:

\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)\) (or equivalently \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)\))
When \(A\) and \(B\) are independent: \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)






In words, when are two events \(A\) and \(B\) independent?
Give two equivalent ways to check independence (formulas or conditions).

Solution:

Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other.
Here are three equivalent ways you just need to give two: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)), or \(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\) (when \(P(A) > 0\)), or \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)

\(P(A \mid B) = 0.3\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)

State the formula for \(P(A \mid B)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)).

Solution:

\(P(A \mid B)\) means the probability that event \(A\) occurs given that event \(B\) has occurred.
\(P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}\)






State the multiplication rule for \(P(A \text{ and } B)\) in terms of \(P(A \mid B)\) and \(P(B)\).
State the special case when \(A\) and \(B\) are independent.

Solution:

\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)\) (or equivalently \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)\))
When \(A\) and \(B\) are independent: \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)






In words, when are two events \(A\) and \(B\) independent?
Give two equivalent ways to check independence (formulas or conditions).

Solution:

Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other.
Here are three equivalent ways you just need to give two: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)), or \(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\) (when \(P(A) > 0\)), or \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)





Write a one-sentence interpretation for each:

\(P(A \mid B) = 0.3\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)

Solution:

Given that \(B\) occurs, the probability that \(A\) occurs is 0.3 (or: there is a 30% chance of \(A\) given \(B\)).
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent.

A fair six-sided die is rolled once.

Find \(P(\text{roll is 2} \mid \text{roll is even})\).
Find \(P(\text{roll is even} \mid \text{roll is 2})\).

Solution:

\(P(\text{2} \mid \text{even}) = \dfrac{P(\text{2 and even})}{P(\text{even})} = \dfrac{P(\text{2})}{P(\text{even})} = \dfrac{1/6}{3/6} = \dfrac{1}{3}\)
\(P(\text{even} \mid \text{2}) = \dfrac{P(\text{even and 2})}{P(\text{2})} = \dfrac{P(\text{2})}{P(\text{2})} = 1\) (since 2 is even)

title: Quiz 4 — Solutions
text:
COVID Setup: A clinic tests a new rapid test for COVID on 1000 people. Of these, 20 have COVID and 980 do not. Among those with COVID, 17 test positive; among those without COVID, 78 test positive.



Solution:

\(P(C) = \dfrac{20}{1000} = 0.02\), \(P(\text{not } C) = \dfrac{980}{1000} = 0.98\)
\(P(T^+ \mid C) = \dfrac{17}{20} = 0.85\) (sensitivity)
\(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } C) = \dfrac{78}{980} \approx 0.0796\) (false positive rate)





Solution:

\(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = \dfrac{17}{1000} = 0.017\)
\(P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+) = \dfrac{78}{1000} = 0.078\)





Solution:

If \(C\) and \(T^+\) were independent: \(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = P(C)\,P(T^+)\). From C4, \(P(T^+) = 0.095\), so this would equal \(0.02 \times 0.095 = 0.0019\).
No, they are not independent. The actual \(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = 0.017\), which is much larger than \(0.0019\). Knowing the test result changes the probability of having COVID (and vice versa).





Solution:
\[
P(T^+) = P(C \text{ and } T^+) + P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+) = \frac{17}{1000} + \frac{78}{1000} = \frac{95}{1000} = 0.095
\]




Solution:
\(P(T^+)\) is the overall proportion of people in this study who tested positive on the rapid test (95 out of 1000, or 9.5%).

title: Lab 3
text:
Purpose: This lab uses simulation in R to estimate probabilities involving conditional probability and the multiplication rule. We build on Lab 1–2 skills: sample(), for loops, if statements, vectors, and mean().
Topics:

Definition of conditional probability
Multiplication rule: \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)\)
Simulation with if statements to count successes
Simulating populations with conditional structure

R functions you’ll use: rep(), sample(), for loop, if statement, vector(), sum(), mean(), which()



A bag contains 3 red and 3 white balls. Two balls are drawn one at a time; the first ball is not replaced before the second is drawn.



Your task: Run code to simulate 10 sets of two draws from the bag. Use "R" and "W" for red and white. Store a 1 in successes[k] when the first draw is white and the second is red.




Your task: Describe what the vector balls contains. How would you modify it for a bag with 5 red and 2 white balls?

Proportion — Plus Four: \(\tilde{p} = (x+2)/(n+4)\), then \(\tilde{p} \pm 2\sqrt{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})/(n+4)}\).

Mean — normal (z) or t-interval: \(\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot s/\sqrt{n}\) (or t.test / equivalent).
Mean — with finite population correction (FPC): Mean interval multiplied by \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\).

title: Lecture 10
text:
Learning Outcomes:

Define random variable and its probability distribution function (pdf) (for discrete random variables).
Define a binomial random variable and a binomial random process.
Match the notation (\(n\), \(x\), \(p\)) and four conditions for a binomial process with the process of tossing a coin.
Express probabilities for binomial random variables via the binomial pdf formula.




A random variable is defined as…













For a discrete random variable \(X\), the probability distribution function (or probability mass function) gives the probability that \(X\) takes each possible value. We write \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for each possible value \(x\).









Example 1. A coin is tossed \(n = 1\) time; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0\) and \(x = 1\).




Example 2. A coin is tossed \(n = 2\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2\).




Example 3. A coin is tossed \(n = 3\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, 3\).




Example 4. A coin is tossed \(n = 4\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\).




General case. A coin is tossed \(n\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\).
.






A binomial random variable \(X\) counts the number of “successes” in a binomial random process.
Four conditions for a binomial random process:

Fixed number of trials \(n\) (e.g., \(n\) coin tosses).
Two outcomes on each trial: “success” or “failure” (e.g., heads vs tails).
Independent trials — the outcome of one trial does not affect the others.
Constant probability \(p\) of success on each trial (e.g., same \(p\) for every toss).

Notation: \(n\) = number of trials, \(p\) = probability of success on one trial, \(X\) = number of successes, and \(x\) = a specific value of \(X\) (0, 1, 2, …, \(n\)).




If \(X\) is binomial with \(n\) trials and success probability \(p\), then for \(x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\),
\[ P(X = x) = f(x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x} = _nC_x\, p^x (1-p)^{n-x} \]
where \(\binom{n}{x} = _nC_x = \dfrac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}\) is the number of ways to choose \(x\) successes out of \(n\) trials.



————————————————————————##

title: Lecture 12
text:
Extra Sensory Perception (ESP) is the ability to accurately predict what will happen using supernatural methods. Do I have it? If so, I could make lots of money by betting on the stock market! To find out, we can take an online ESP test and then analyze the data using the Binomial Test.




We will collect data from the Advanced ESP (Zener Cards) test at PsychicScience.org.

The test uses 5 possible symbols (circle, cross, waves, square, star).
On each trial, the computer randomly selects one symbol; your task is to guess it.
We will use 25 attempts (25 cards).
Choose: Clairvoyance, Open deck, Cards seen, and 25 cards.





Let \(p\) be the probability of a correct prediction.

If I don’t have ESP, I’m just guessing among 5 options, so \(p = 1/5 = 0.2\).
If I do have ESP, I should get more than chance, so \(p > 0.2\).

In conventional notation:
\[H_0: p = 0.2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_a: p > 0.2\]




When we record the number of correct predictions out of 25 attempts, we treat this as a Binomial Random Process. We check the 4 conditions:

Binary outcome: Each prediction was either correct or not.
Fixed number of trials: We made a fixed number of attempts, \(n = 25\).
Fixed probability: The probability of a correct guess on each trial doesn’t change (open deck: each card is chosen at random from 5 options each time).
Independent trials: We don’t use information from previous attempts to inform the next guess (and the computer randomizes each card independently).

So the number of correct predictions in 25 attempts can be modeled by a binomial random variable with \(n = 25\) and \(p = 0.2\) under the null hypothesis.

title: Lecture 14
text:
For each problem, follow these steps:

State the research question.
Define the parameter of interest, \(p\), in words.
State the null and alternative hypotheses using appropriate symbols.
Identify \(n\) and the value of \(p\) under \(H_0\) (for the null distribution).
Verify that the 4 conditions of a Binomial Random Process (BRP) are satisfied by the data collection.
Assuming the null hypothesis is true, create a plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, n, p)\) (the null distribution), e.g. by hand or with software. Do not shade yet — shading will be done by hand in the direction(s) of the alternative.
Identify the observed value of \(x\) from the dataset.
On the plot, add a vertical line at the observed \(x\) and shade the bar(s) in the direction(s) of the inequality in \(H_a\) (by hand).
Compute the p-value: the probability of observing data as or more extreme than the observed \(x\) in the direction of \(H_a\), assuming \(H_0\) is true. (For a two-sided \(H_a\), use both tails.)
Make a conclusion in context (reject \(H_0\) or fail to reject \(H_0\), and what that means for the research question).




Research question: Is the proportion of left-handed students at our school higher than the national rate of 10%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student from our school is left-handed (i.e., the proportion of left-handers in the population we are sampling).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.10\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.10\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.10\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (left-handed or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Plot: Create a bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.10)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). Leave unshaded; then by hand add a vertical line at \(x = 6\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 6\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.10
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 6 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 5)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(5, 25, 0.10) Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 6\) were left-handed.\(\approx 0.03\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with a 10% left-handed rate; we have evidence that the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than 10%.




Research question: Is the proportion of defective items from the new supplier less than 15%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly selected item from the new supplier is defective (i.e., the proportion of defectives in the process).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.15\) vs \(H_a: p < 0.15\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) items, \(p = 0.15\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (defective or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent items.
Data: In a sample of 25 items, \(x = 3\) were defective.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.15)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 3\) and shade the bars for \(x \leq 3\) (left tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.15
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 3 \mid H_0) = \text{pbinom}(3, 25, 0.15)\) \(\approx 0.47\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 15% defective rate; we do not have evidence that the proportion of defectives from the new supplier is less than 15%.




Research question: Are the two dice fair, in terms of the probability that the sum is 7 or 11? (With fair dice, that probability is \(8/36\): 6 ways to sum to 7 plus 2 ways to sum to 11, out of 36 equally likely outcomes.)
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a single roll of two dice gives a sum of 7 or 11.
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 8/36\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 8/36\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) rolls of the two dice, \(p = 8/36\) (under the null that the dice are fair).
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (sum is 7 or 11, or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent rolls.
Data: In 25 rolls of the two dice, \(x = 2\) times the sum was 7 or 11 (far from the expected \(25 \times (8/36) \approx 5.6\) under \(H_0\)).
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 8/36)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 2\) and shade both tails: bars for \(x \leq 2\) and for \(x \geq 9\) (direction of “more extreme than” for a two-sided alternative). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 8/36
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 2) + P(X \geq 9)\) under \(H_0\). For example, pbinom(2, 25, 8/36) + (1 - pbinom(8, 25, 8/36)) \(\approx 0.04\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with fair dice having probability \(8/36\) for sum 7 or 11; we have evidence that \(p \neq 8/36\).




Research question: Do more than 20% of students at our school use the gym at least once per week?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student uses the gym at least once per week (i.e., the proportion of such students in the population).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.20\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.20\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.20\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (uses gym weekly or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 5\) use the gym at least once per week.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.20)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 5\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 5\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.20
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 5 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 4)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(4, 25, 0.20) \(\approx 0.58\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 20% gym-use rate; we do not have evidence that more than 20% of students use the gym at least once per week.

Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a single roll of two dice gives a sum of 7 or 11.
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 8/36\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 8/36\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) rolls of the two dice, \(p = 8/36\) (under the null that the dice are fair).
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (sum is 7 or 11, or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent rolls.
Data: In 25 rolls of the two dice, \(x = 2\) times the sum was 7 or 11 (far from the expected \(25 \times (8/36) \approx 5.6\) under \(H_0\)).
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 8/36)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 2\) and shade both tails: bars for \(x \leq 2\) and for \(x \geq 9\) (direction of “more extreme than” for a two-sided alternative). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 8/36
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 2) + P(X \geq 9)\) under \(H_0\). For example, pbinom(2, 25, 8/36) + (1 - pbinom(8, 25, 8/36)) \(\approx 0.04\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with fair dice having probability \(8/36\) for sum 7 or 11; we have evidence that \(p \neq 8/36\).

title: Lecture 14
text:
Research question: Do more than 20% of students at our school use the gym at least once per week?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student uses the gym at least once per week (i.e., the proportion of such students in the population).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.20\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.20\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.20\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (uses gym weekly or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 5\) use the gym at least once per week.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.20)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 5\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 5\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.20
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 5 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 4)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(4, 25, 0.20) \(\approx 0.58\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 20% gym-use rate; we do not have evidence that more than 20% of students use the gym at least once per week.

title: Lecture 19
text:
Discuss the potential for and impact of bias given a description of a sampling method.
Identify ways to reduce bias and increase precision when sampling.
Identify a sampling frame for a given population.
Carry out simple random sampling.
Classify a sampling method as convenience, simple random, cluster, systematic, stratified, or multi-stage.





A confidence interval (CI) provides a range of plausible values for a population parameter based on a sample from the population. It quantifies the uncertainty in our sample estimate. For a 95% CI, we use the approximate formula:
Statistic ± 2 × (standard error of the statistic).
Whether that interval is actually reasonable for the population we care about depends on how the sample was obtained — that is, on the sampling method.




Suppose we want to estimate the proportion of all undergraduate students on campus who are business majors.
We decide to survey the students in this Stat 109 class.

In class: How many students in the room are majoring in business? (Count; call that \(x\). The class size is \(n\).)
Example values: Suppose there are \(n = 25\) students and \(x = 8\) report being business majors.

Sample proportion: \(\hat{p} = \frac{x}{n} = \frac{8}{25} = 0.32\) (32%).
95% confidence interval for \(p\) (Wald, assuming conditions hold):

\(\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.32 \times 0.68}{25}} \approx 0.093\).
95% CI: \(\hat{p} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\hat{p}) = 0.32 \pm 0.186\), so about [0.13, 0.51] or [13%, 51%].

So from the formula we get: We are 95% confident that the true proportion of all undergraduates who are business majors is between about 13% and 51%.




Critical question: Is it reasonable to treat this interval as applying to all students on campus?

Population of interest: All undergraduate students at the university.
Sample: Only students in this Stat 109 class.

Problem: Business majors are not required to take Stat 109. So:

The students in this class are not a random sample of all undergraduates.
We have a convenience sample (whoever is in the room). The proportion of business majors in Stat 109 might be higher or lower than on the whole campus — we have no guarantee the sample is representative.
So the confidence interval from the formula may be misleading: it describes uncertainty due to random sampling variation in using \(\hat{p}\) to estimate \(p\), but it does not account for bias due to how we chose the sample. If the sample is biased, the interval may not be a plausible range for the campus proportion at all.

Takeaway: The way we sample matters. To make confidence intervals that we can trust for the population we care about, we need to think about sampling methods and bias.




The sampling frame is the list we use to identify and select units from the population. Ideally, the frame matches the population of interest (every member has a chance to be selected; no one is in the frame who isn’t in the population, and vice versa). In the business-major example, “students in this Stat 109 class” is a poor frame for “all undergraduates on campus”.




We can classify how a sample was obtained:







Method
Brief description




Convenience
Select whoever is easy to reach (e.g. this class). Often biased.


Simple random (SRS)
Every subset of \(n\) units has the same chance of being selected.


Stratified
Divide population into strata (groups); take a random sample from each stratum.


Cluster
Divide population into clusters; randomly select clusters and often measure all (or a sample of) units in those clusters.


Systematic
From a list, take every \(k\)th unit (e.g. every 10th student).


Multi-stage
Combine methods (e.g. randomly select clusters, then randomly select units within clusters).



Lecture 20 will go into more detail on these and on carrying out simple random sampling.





Bias: Systematic tendency to over- or under-estimate the parameter. Comes from how we select or measure (e.g. who is in the frame, who responds).
Precision: How similar our estimates would be if we repeated the process (related to standard error and width of the CI). Affected by sample size and design.

Types of bias (when sampling or surveying):

Sampling bias: The sampling method systematically excludes or over-represents some groups (e.g. convenience sample of one class).
Undercoverage: Some groups in the population are not in the sampling frame or have little chance of being selected.
Nonresponse bias: People who don’t respond differ from those who do.
Response bias: The way questions are asked or the setting leads to systematically wrong answers.
Voluntary response bias: Only people who choose to respond are included; they often differ from the population.

Ways to reduce bias: Use a good sampling frame, avoid convenience/voluntary samples when possible, use random sampling (e.g. SRS, stratified), try to reduce nonresponse and careful questionnaire design to reduce response bias.
Ways to increase precision: Increase sample size; sometimes use stratified or other designs that reduce variability.




So far we assumed the sample is a small fraction of the population, so we used:

Proportion: \(\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\)
Mean: \(\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\)

When we sample without replacement from a finite population of size \(N\), and \(n\) is not negligible compared to \(N\), we multiply the standard error by the finite population correction (FPC):
\[\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}.\]
(When \(N\) is large, \(\sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N}}\) is a common approximation.)
Interpretation: When we draw a large fraction of the population, we get more information than the “infinite population” formula suggests, so the standard error shrinks (the FPC is \(< 1\) when \(n > 1\)). When \(n\) is small relative to \(N\), the FPC is close to 1 and we usually ignore it.
95% confidence interval for a proportion (with FPC):
\[\hat{p} \pm 2 \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}.\]
95% confidence interval for a mean (with FPC):
\[\bar{x} \pm 2 \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}.\]
When to use: When the population is finite and \(n/N\) is large (e.g. \(n\) is more than about 5–10% of \(N\)), the FPC narrows the interval. When \(n/N\) is small, the FPC is near 1 and the usual formulas are fine.




Setting: A small clinic has \(N = 50\) patients who had a certain procedure. We take a random sample of \(n = 20\) and find \(x = 8\) had a positive outcome. So \(\hat{p} = 8/20 = 0.40\).
Without FPC (infinite-population formula):

\(\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{0.40 \times 0.60}{20}} = \sqrt{0.012} \approx 0.1096\).
Margin \(\approx 2(0.1096) = 0.219\). 95% CI: \(0.40 \pm 0.219\) → about [0.18, 0.62].

With FPC:

\(\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{50-20}{49}} = \sqrt{\frac{30}{49}} \approx 0.782\).
\(\text{SE}(\hat{p}) \times \text{FPC} \approx 0.1096 \times 0.782 \approx 0.0857\). Margin \(\approx 2(0.0857) = 0.171\).
95% CI (with FPC): \(0.40 \pm 0.171\) → about [0.23, 0.57].

The FPC narrows the interval because we sampled a large fraction (20/50 = 40%) of the population, so we have more information than the infinite-population formula assumes.




Setting: A small population of \(N = 30\) plots. We randomly select \(n = 10\) and measure a variable (e.g. plant count per plot). The data are: 4, 5, 6, 7, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12.

Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = (4+5+6+7+7+8+9+10+11+12)/10 = 79/10 = 7.9\).
Sample SD: Deviations from 7.9: \(-3.9\), \(-2.9\), \(-1.9\), \(-0.9\), \(-0.9\), \(0.1\), \(1.1\), \(2.1\), \(3.1\), \(4.1\). Squared: 15.21, 8.41, 3.61, 0.81, 0.81, 0.01, 1.21, 4.41, 9.61, 16.81. Sum = 60.9. \(s^2 = 60.9/9 \approx 6.77\), so \(s \approx 2.60\).
\(\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = s/\sqrt{n} = 2.60/\sqrt{10} \approx 0.822\).

Without FPC (infinite-population formula):

Margin \(\approx 2(0.822) = 1.64\). 95% CI: \(7.9 \pm 1.64\) → about [6.3, 9.5].

With FPC:

\(\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{30-10}{29}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}} \approx 0.830\).
\(\text{SE}(\bar{x}) \times \text{FPC} \approx 0.822 \times 0.830 \approx 0.682\). Margin \(\approx 2(0.682) = 1.36\).
95% CI (with FPC): \(7.9 \pm 1.36\) → about [6.5, 9.3].

Again, the FPC gives a slightly narrower interval because we sampled a substantial fraction (10/30 \(\approx\) 33%) of the finite population.




When the population is treated as infinite (or \(n/N\) is very small), use these versions and conditions:
One proportion (Wald), 95% CI: \(\hat{p} \pm 2 \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\).
Conditions:

Binomial random process: (a) Binary outcome, (b) Independent trials, (c) Number of trials \(n\) fixed, (d) Same probability \(p\) on each trial.
Normal approximation: \(n\hat{p} \geq 10\) and \(n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 10\).

One mean, 95% CI: \(\bar{x} \pm 2 \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\) (or use \(t\) for small \(n\)).
Conditions:

Either the population is (approximately) normal, or \(n > 30\) (so the sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) is approximately normal by the CLT).
The sample is a random sample from the population (or can be treated as such).

When the population is finite and \(n/N\) is not small, multiply the margin of error by the FPC \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\) for both the proportion and the mean.

title: Lecture 20
text:
Identify a sampling frame for a given population.
Carry out simple random sampling (e.g. using a list and random numbers).
Classify a sampling method as convenience, simple random, cluster, systematic, stratified, or multi-stage.
Discuss the potential for bias and how to reduce it; identify ways to increase precision.





The sampling frame is the list or procedure from which we actually select units. For a given population (e.g. “all undergraduates,” “all patients at a clinic”), we need to ask: What list or mechanism will we use to choose who gets into the sample? The frame should match the population as closely as possible. Gaps (undercoverage) or duplicates can cause bias.
Example: To sample undergraduates, the frame might be a registrar’s list of all enrolled students (with IDs). “Students in Stat 109” is a poor frame for “all undergraduates.”




In simple random sampling, every possible subset of \(n\) units from the population (or from the frame) has the same chance of being the sample. Equivalently, every unit has the same probability of being selected, and selections are made at random.
How to carry out SRS (by hand or with software):

Get a list of all units in the sampling frame (numbered 1 to \(N\)).
Generate random numbers in the range \(1\) to \(N\) (e.g. using a random number generator, table, or software). Draw units corresponding to those numbers. If sampling without replacement, skip repeats until you have \(n\) distinct units.

Example: To select 5 students from a class of 25, number students 1–25. Use a random number generator (or similar) set to “integers from 1 to 25,” generate numbers, and take the students with those IDs. If a number repeats, draw again until you have 5 distinct IDs.




Given a description of how a sample was obtained, classify it as one of:







Method
What happens




Convenience
Units chosen because they are easy to access (e.g. “people in this room,” “first 50 visitors”). Often not representative.


Simple random (SRS)
Every set of \(n\) units from the frame has the same chance of being selected; usually via random numbers.


Stratified
Population divided into strata (e.g. by major, by year); a random sample is taken from each stratum. Improves representation of each group.


Cluster
Population divided into clusters (e.g. schools, wards); we randomly select clusters and then observe all (or a random sample of) units in those clusters. Useful when listing individuals is hard but listing clusters is easy.


Systematic
From an ordered list, pick a random start, then take every \(k\)th unit (e.g. every 10th name).


Multi-stage
Several stages of random selection (e.g. randomly choose schools, then randomly choose students within those schools).







For each scenario below: (1) Classify the sampling method as random sampling (e.g. SRS, stratified, cluster) or convenience (or other as appropriate). (2) Impact on generalizability: Would results generalize well to the population of interest? Why or why not? (3) How to reduce bias and how to increase precision (one concrete suggestion for each, if applicable).



A student group wants to estimate the proportion of all undergraduates who support building a new recreation center. They stand outside the student union from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. on two weekdays and ask every 10th person who walks by to complete a short survey. They get 120 responses.

Classify: Is this random sampling or convenience (or something else)? What is the sampling frame?
Impact on generalizability: Who might be over- or under-represented? Can we generalize the sample proportion to all undergraduates?
Reduce bias: How could the study be redesigned to reduce bias (e.g. who is included)?
Increase precision: What could be done to increase precision of the estimate?





A university wants to estimate the mean satisfaction score (1–5) for all students who took at least one online course last year. They send an email survey to a random sample of 500 students drawn from the registrar’s list of all students who were enrolled in at least one online course; 180 students respond.

Classify: Is the selection of the 500 random or convenience? What about the 180 who responded?
Impact on generalizability: Even though the initial 500 were a random sample, might the 180 respondents differ from the 320 non-respondents? How does that affect generalizability?
Reduce bias: What type of bias is most relevant here (e.g. nonresponse)? How could bias be reduced?
Increase precision: How could precision be increased (e.g. sample size, design)?





A hospital wants to estimate the proportion of patients who are “very satisfied” with their care. They hand out a satisfaction survey to every patient who is discharged from the maternity ward during a 3-month period and receives the survey before leaving; about 70% return the completed survey.

Classify: How were patients selected? Is this random sampling or convenience?
Impact on generalizability: To what population would the sample proportion generalize? To all hospital patients? To all maternity patients?
Reduce bias: What biases might be present (e.g. undercoverage, nonresponse)? Suggest one way to reduce bias.
Increase precision: Suggest one way to increase precision.







Reduce bias: Use a frame that covers the population; avoid convenience and voluntary response when possible; use random sampling (SRS, stratified, etc.); minimize nonresponse and design surveys to reduce response bias.
Increase precision: Increase sample size; use designs that reduce variability (e.g. stratified sampling when groups differ a lot).






A confidence interval is only as good as the sample. Bad sampling (e.g. convenience for a campus-wide question) can make the interval misleading even if the math is correct.
Sampling frame = list or procedure used to select units. Should align with the population of interest.
SRS = every subset of size \(n\) equally likely; carried out using a list and random numbers.
Classify methods as convenience, SRS, stratified, cluster, systematic, or multi-stage.
Bias (sampling, undercoverage, nonresponse, response, voluntary response) can invalidate inference; precision is improved by larger \(n\) and sometimes by design (e.g. stratification).


Labs 8 and beyond will practice identifying sampling methods, carrying out SRS, and interpreting confidence intervals in light of how the sample was obtained.

title: Lecture 20
text:
Identify a sampling frame for a given population.
Carry out simple random sampling (e.g. using a list and random numbers).
Classify a sampling method as convenience, simple random, cluster, systematic, stratified, or multi-stage.
Discuss the potential for bias and how to reduce it; identify ways to increase precision.

title: Lecture 20
text:
The sampling frame is the list or procedure from which we actually select units. For a given population (e.g. “all undergraduates,” “all patients at a clinic”), we need to ask: What list or mechanism will we use to choose who gets into the sample? The frame should match the population as closely as possible. Gaps (undercoverage) or duplicates can cause bias.
Example: To sample undergraduates, the frame might be a registrar’s list of all enrolled students (with IDs). “Students in Stat 109” is a poor frame for “all undergraduates.”

title: Lecture 20
text:
In simple random sampling, every possible subset of \(n\) units from the population (or from the frame) has the same chance of being the sample. Equivalently, every unit has the same probability of being selected, and selections are made at random.
How to carry out SRS (by hand or with software):

Get a list of all units in the sampling frame (numbered 1 to \(N\)).
Generate random numbers in the range \(1\) to \(N\) (e.g. using a random number generator, table, or software). Draw units corresponding to those numbers. If sampling without replacement, skip repeats until you have \(n\) distinct units.

Example: To select 5 students from a class of 25, number students 1–25. Use a random number generator (or similar) set to “integers from 1 to 25,” generate numbers, and take the students with those IDs. If a number repeats, draw again until you have 5 distinct IDs.

title: Lecture 20
text:
Given a description of how a sample was obtained, classify it as one of:







Method
What happens




Convenience
Units chosen because they are easy to access (e.g. “people in this room,” “first 50 visitors”). Often not representative.


Simple random (SRS)
Every set of \(n\) units from the frame has the same chance of being selected; usually via random numbers.


Stratified
Population divided into strata (e.g. by major, by year); a random sample is taken from each stratum. Improves representation of each group.


Cluster
Population divided into clusters (e.g. schools, wards); we randomly select clusters and then observe all (or a random sample of) units in those clusters. Useful when listing individuals is hard but listing clusters is easy.


Systematic
From an ordered list, pick a random start, then take every \(k\)th unit (e.g. every 10th name).


Multi-stage
Several stages of random selection (e.g. randomly choose schools, then randomly choose students within those schools).

title: Lecture 20
text:
For each scenario below: (1) Classify the sampling method as random sampling (e.g. SRS, stratified, cluster) or convenience (or other as appropriate). (2) Impact on generalizability: Would results generalize well to the population of interest? Why or why not? (3) How to reduce bias and how to increase precision (one concrete suggestion for each, if applicable).



A student group wants to estimate the proportion of all undergraduates who support building a new recreation center. They stand outside the student union from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. on two weekdays and ask every 10th person who walks by to complete a short survey. They get 120 responses.

Classify: Is this random sampling or convenience (or something else)? What is the sampling frame?
Impact on generalizability: Who might be over- or under-represented? Can we generalize the sample proportion to all undergraduates?
Reduce bias: How could the study be redesigned to reduce bias (e.g. who is included)?
Increase precision: What could be done to increase precision of the estimate?





A university wants to estimate the mean satisfaction score (1–5) for all students who took at least one online course last year. They send an email survey to a random sample of 500 students drawn from the registrar’s list of all students who were enrolled in at least one online course; 180 students respond.

Classify: Is the selection of the 500 random or convenience? What about the 180 who responded?
Impact on generalizability: Even though the initial 500 were a random sample, might the 180 respondents differ from the 320 non-respondents? How does that affect generalizability?
Reduce bias: What type of bias is most relevant here (e.g. nonresponse)? How could bias be reduced?
Increase precision: How could precision be increased (e.g. sample size, design)?





A hospital wants to estimate the proportion of patients who are “very satisfied” with their care. They hand out a satisfaction survey to every patient who is discharged from the maternity ward during a 3-month period and receives the survey before leaving; about 70% return the completed survey.

Classify: How were patients selected? Is this random sampling or convenience?
Impact on generalizability: To what population would the sample proportion generalize? To all hospital patients? To all maternity patients?

title: Lecture 3
text:
Probability calculations the “long way”



Define and identify the following terms for a given trial:

Sample space
Outcome
Event

Use appropriate notation for the probability of an event, \(P(A)\)
State and use the formula for the probability of an event if the outcomes in the sample space are equally likely.





Let’s define the following terms:

Sample space: the set of all possible outcomes of a trial, conventional notation \(S\)
Event: a set of one or more outcomes of a trial, usually notated with an uppercase letter, e.g. \(A\) or \(B\) or a meaningful letter such as \(H\) for “heads”





Fill in the meaning or notation for each of the following:
Suppose that a single six-sided die is rolled.

\(S\) = __________________________________
Let _____ = be the event that the die lands with the “5” or the “6” side up
Not \(A\) means _______________________________________
Let \(B\) be the event that the die is an even number
\(A\) and \(B\) is the set __________________________________
\(A\) or \(B\) is the set _______________________________________
\(A\) given \(B\) means “assuming that _____________ happened, that the event ________ also happened”





Translate the following “sentences” to English:

\(P(A) = 0.7\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = 0.2\)
\(P(\text{not } A) = 0.1\)
\(P(A|B) = 0.25\)





Fill in the blanks:

For any event \(A\), ____________\(P(A)\) ____________
\(P(S) =\) ____________
For two non-overlapping events \(A\) and \(B\), \(P(A \text{ or } B) =\) ____________


Note: “axiom” is a type of rule that we must merely “agree” is true, rather than being able to “prove” that it is true.

Translate the following sentence to correct probability notation:
“The chance of it raining in the next 10 minutes is 80%”







If all outcomes in the Sample Space \(S\) of a trial are equally likely, then
\[P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|}\]
where \(A\) is any event that is contained in \(S\), and the notation \(|\text{set}|\) is the number of outcomes in the set.




Suppose a fair coin is tossed 2 times.

List the sample space \(S\) of this experiment
List the outcomes in the sample space that are in the event \(B =\) “the coin lands heads at least once”
Find \(P(B)\).











Suppose a fair die is rolled twice.

List the sample space \(S\) of this experiment
List the outcomes in the sample space that are in the event \(C =\) “the sum of the two rolls is 7”
Find \(P(C)\)

text:
Lecture 5: Addition Rule for Probability
Learning Outcomes:

State the Addition Rule for any two events
Use the addition rule to compute the probability of events A or B, being aware of special cases.


Addition Rule
For any two events A and B…
Visualizing the Addition Rule with a Venn Diagram
Example: Suppose a class consists of 20 students.  Of these students, 12 have backpacks and 3 have hats.  There is only 1 student with both a backpack and a hat. 
Suppose one student is randomly selected from the 20.  What is the probability this student will either have a backpack OR have a hat?
Practice Problem: Suppose a class consists of 30 students.  Of these students, 8 like to go sky diving and 20 like to go surfing.  There are 7 students that like both sky diving and surfing.
Suppose one student is randomly selected from the 30.  What is the probability this student will like sky diving OR surfing

Special Cases of the Addition Rule
Complement Rule
Non-overlapping Events
Example: Suppose the probability of it snowing at least once this November is 0.83.  what is the probability that it will not snow at all this November?
Example: Suppose that a brand of gummy vitamins come in three animal shapes: Lions, tigers or bears .  The company creates the gummies as follows: 30% of the shapes are bears, 30% are tigers and 40% are lions. 
What is the probability that a randomly selected gummy will be either a bear or a tiger?

title: Lecture 6
text:
Lecture 6: Conditional Probability & Multiplication Rule
Learning Outcomes:

State the definition of conditional probability and compute \(P(A \mid B)\).
Use the multiplication rule to compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Determine whether two events are independent (using equivalent criteria).


Conditional Probability
Notation: \(P(A \mid B)\) means “the probability event \(A\) occurs given event \(B\) occurred.”
Definition: For events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\),
\[
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}
\]



Example 1 (die roll): Consider the trial of rolling a fair six-sided die once.
Let \(A\) be the event “the roll is a 3” and let \(B\) be the event “the roll is odd.”

List the outcomes in \(A\), in \(B\), and in \(A \text{ and } B\).
Compute \(P(A)\), \(P(B)\), and \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Find \(P(A \mid B)\): the probability of getting a 3 given the roll resulted in an odd number.
Find \(P(B \mid A)\): the probability of getting an odd number given the roll resulted in a 3.




Practice Problem 1: A jar contains 10 candies: 6 are red and 4 are blue. Two candies are selected without replacement.
Let \(A\) be the event “the first candy is red” and \(B\) be the event “the second candy is red.”

Find \(P(A)\).
Find \(P(B \mid A)\).
Use the multiplication rule (below) to find \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).





Multiplication Rule
For any two events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\),
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)
\]
Equivalently (if \(P(A) > 0\)),
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
\]



Example 2 (two cards, no replacement): A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck, then a second card is drawn without replacement.
Find the probability the first card is an Ace and the second card is a King.




Independence
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other.
Equivalent ways to check independence:

\(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\))
\(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\) (when \(P(A) > 0\))
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)




Example 3 (king vs spade): Suppose a card is randomly dealt from a standard 52-card deck.
Is the event “the card is a king” independent of the event “the card is a spade”?




Multiplication Rule for Two Independent Events
If \(A\) and \(B\) are independent, then
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)
\]



Example 4 (two coin tosses): Suppose a fair coin is tossed twice.
Find the probability of getting “heads” on the first toss and “tails” on the second toss.



Practice Problem 2: Suppose a fair six-sided die is rolled twice.
Let \(A\) be the event “the first roll is even” and let \(B\) be the event “the second roll is 6.”

Are \(A\) and \(B\) independent? (Explain briefly.)
Compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).

(c) The proportion of people who would both have the disease and receive a positive test result.


\(P(B \mid A)\)
(d) Among people who have the disease, the chance the test correctly identifies them as positive.


\(P(A \text{ or } B)\)
(e) The chance that at least one of two things happens: the person has the disease, or tests positive (or both).



Write your matches (e.g., \(P(A) \to\) (a)).




A fair six-sided die is rolled once.

Find \(P(\text{roll is 2} \mid \text{roll is even})\).
Find \(P(\text{roll is even} \mid \text{roll is 2})\).








A jar contains 10 candies: 6 are red and 4 are blue. Two candies are selected without replacement.
Let \(A\) = “first candy is red” and \(B\) = “second candy is red.”

Find \(P(A)\).
Find \(P(B \mid A)\).
Use the multiplication rule to find \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).





A bag contains 8 marbles: 4 are blue and 4 are green. Two marbles are drawn one at a time; the first is not replaced before the second is drawn.
Find the probability the first marble is blue and the second marble is green.




A bag contains 6 tiles: 2 red, 2 blue, and 2 green. Each tile is either round or square — there are 3 round and 3 square in total (one of each color-shape combination). One tile is drawn at random.
Is the event “the tile is red” independent of the event “the tile is round”? Show your reasoning.




Suppose a fair coin is tossed twice.
Find the probability of getting “heads” on the first toss and “tails” on the second toss.




A bag contains 8 marbles: 4 are blue and 4 are green. Two marbles are drawn one at a time without replacement.
Given that the first marble is blue, find the probability the second marble is green. (That is, find \(P(\text{second is green} \mid \text{first is blue})\).)




Suppose a fair six-sided die is rolled twice.
Let \(A\) = “first roll is even” and \(B\) = “second roll is 6.”

Are \(A\) and \(B\) independent? Explain briefly.
Compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).






COVID Setup: A clinic tests a new rapid test for COVID on 1000 people. Of these, 20 have COVID and 980 do not. Among those with COVID, 17 test positive; among those without COVID, 78 test positive.
Let \(C\) = “has COVID” and \(T^+\) = “tests positive.”




Find \(P(C)\) and \(P(\text{not } C)\) for a randomly selected person from the 1000.
Find \(P(T^+ \mid C)\) (sensitivity).
Find \(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } C)\) (false positive rate).






Find \(P(C \text{ and } T^+)\).
Find \(P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+)\).






If \(C\) and \(T^+\) were independent, what would \(P(C \text{ and } T^+)\) equal?
Are \(C\) and \(T^+\) actually independent? Explain briefly using the definition.





Using the COVID data, find \(P(T^+)\) — the overall probability of a positive test.




In one sentence, interpret what \(P(T^+)\) means in the context of this COVID study.







Write R code that creates a vector balls representing a bag with 5 red and 2 white balls. Use "R" and "W" as labels.




A bag has 3 red and 3 white balls. Write complete R code that simulates 10,000 draws of 2 balls (without replacement), records a success when the first is white and the second is red, and estimates the probability with mean(successes).




Suppose successes is a numeric vector of length 10,000 from a simulation, where 1 = success and 0 = no success.
Write one line of R code to find which replicate numbers had a success.




The code below estimates \(P(\text{white first and red second})\) when drawing 2 balls from a bag of 3 red and 3 white.
Your task: Cross out the part that needs to change and write the modification so the code instead estimates \(P(\text{exactly one red})\) — i.e., either (R then W) or (W then R). Use the logical operator | for “or”.
balls <- rep(c("R", "W"), c(3, 3))
replicates <- 10000
successes <- vector("numeric", replicates)
set.seed(2018)
for (k in 1:replicates) {
  draw <- sample(balls, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  if (draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R") {
    successes[k] <- 1
  }
}
mean(successes)




Write R code that simulates 10,000 individuals where 70% like eggnog and 30% do not. Use sample() with the prob argument to specify these probabilities, and store the result in eggnog.




A population of 10,000 was simulated with: \(P(\text{disease}) = 0.01\); among those with disease, \(P(\text{positive}) = 0.95\); among those without disease, \(P(\text{positive}) = 0.10\). The results are stored in vectors disease (0 = no, 1 = yes) and test_result (0 = negative, 1 = positive).Write one line of R code to estimate \(P(D \text{ and positive})\) — the proportion of individuals who have the disease and a positive test. Use sum() and &.————————————————————————
